# Course overview
## Audience and prerequisites
## Learning objectives
## Course map
## How to use this book

# Motivation: modelling binary outcomes
## Link back to the Linear Regression Short Course
## Examples of binary outcomes
## Coding pass/fail as 0/1
## Expected value of a binary outcome as a probability
## Why ordinary linear regression is not appropriate
## Probability, odds, log-odds, and the logistic function

# From linear models to fitting logistic regression
## Linear regression as a mean model plus an error model
## The Bernoulli/binomial error model for binary outcomes
## The GLM framework
## Logistic regression as a binomial GLM with a logit link
## The `glm(..., family = binomial)` formula in R
## Maximum likelihood estimation
## Estimated coefficients versus true parameters
## Coefficients on the log-odds scale
## Odds ratios are not probability differences
## Odds ratios and confidence intervals
## Adding multiple predictors and categorical predictors

# Assessing logistic regression models
## Likelihood and deviance
## Likelihood ratio tests for nested models
## AIC and model complexity
## Pseudo R-squared
## Residuals, influence, and common problems

# Predictions from logistic regression models
## Predictions on the link scale and probability scale
## Scenario-based predicted probabilities
## Classification thresholds
## Confusion matrices and classification metrics
## ROC curves and AUC

# Model building and mini case study
## Define the event and event rate
## Choose interpretable predictors
## Compare candidate models
## Consider interactions carefully
## Communicate with odds ratios and predicted probabilities
## Report limitations and performance
